hero.desc: Senior scientist and associate researcher at the Rattan Lal Center for Carbon Management at The Ohio State University — a world reference in soil organic carbon dynamics, the No-Till System, and regenerative agriculture in the tropics.

perfil.title: Background & Training
perfil.lead: Over 40 years dedicated to research in conservation soil management, training researchers across 11 countries and co-author of 25 articles with Nobel Peace Prize laureate Prof. Rattan Lal.
perfil.instValue: Rattan Lal Center for Carbon Management — The Ohio State University
perfil.bio1: Prof. Juca Sá is one of the world's leading experts in soil organic carbon dynamics in No-Till systems. His scientific journey began in 1981, shortly after graduating in Agronomic Engineering from UFRRJ, passing through pioneering No-Till institutions such as Fundação ABC, UEPG (State University of Ponta Grossa) — where he worked from 1996 to 2019 — and today as a senior associate researcher at The Ohio State University, alongside Nobel Peace Prize laureate Prof. Rattan Lal.
perfil.bio2: His research has demonstrated that No-Till, when correctly implemented across its three pillars, can restore soil organic carbon stocks to levels comparable to native vegetation. A 2025 study co-authored with Rattan Lal — covering 63 farms across the Cerrado and Atlantic Forest biomes — confirmed that 25% of properties already exceed carbon stocks of native vegetation.
perfil.bio3: He has mentored 32 undergraduate students, 12 master's, 3 doctoral, and 7 post-doctoral researchers, and trained researchers from 11 countries across Asia and Africa in 7 editions of international courses on soil organic matter management.

timeline.text: B.Sc. in Agronomic Engineering — Federal Rural University of Rio de Janeiro. Began career at the Dairy Farmers Cooperative of Paraná.
Soil fertility researcher — Fundação ABC, a pioneering No-Till institution in Brazil. First deep engagement with the NT system.
M.Sc. (UFPR) and Ph.D. in Soils — ESALQ/USP with a sandwich period at The Ohio State University. First international publications on carbon sequestration in Brazilian Oxisols.
Associate Professor C — Dept. of Soil Science and Agricultural Engineering, UEPG. Built one of Brazil's leading research groups in soil organic matter.
Post-doctorates (2008, 2012) and Senior Visiting Professor (2015) at C-MASC (Carbon Management and Sequestration Center), alongside Prof. Rattan Lal.
Opening speaker at the House of Sustainable Agriculture — presented groundbreaking NT carbon sequestration results at the UN COP30.

impacto.title: Numbers That Transform
impacto.lead: Measurable outcomes of the No-Till System according to Prof. Juca Sá's research.

opinioes.title: The 3 Pillars of the No-Till System
opinioes.lead: According to Prof. Juca Sá, 85–90% of "no-till" areas in Brazil do not correctly apply the three fundamental principles — and this compromises all the benefits of the system.

principios.descricao: The soil is disturbed only at the seeding row. No conventional plowing or harrowing. The natural structure and soil aggregates are preserved.
Living plants or straw throughout the year. Requires 16–19 t/ha of biomass/straw to ensure continuous protection and feed soil biota year-round.
Diversity of plant species in rotation to increase quality and quantity of biomass-C, stimulate edaphic biota, and break pest and disease cycles.

citacoes.texto: Crop residues are the food of the soil, and by feeding the soil, we feed humans — and agriculture can contribute to minimizing the climate crisis.
citacoes.fonte: Prof. Juca Sá — FEBRAPDP / COP30, Belém 2025

citacoes.texto: The No-Till System, grounded in its principles, has the capacity to recover carbon, reaching levels similar to those of native vegetation.
citacoes.fonte: Science of the Total Environment, 2025 (Sá & Lal)

citacoes.texto: Expanding one hectare of correct no-till can offset the preservation of one hectare of forest in terms of carbon accounting.
citacoes.fonte: Interview — Notícias Agrícolas / 17th National NT Congress

citacoes.texto: Full implementation of NT could mitigate 211 million tons of CO₂-eq per year in Brazil — more than 10% of all agribusiness emissions.
